verses.text: And Ahab had seventy sons in Samaria. And Jehu wrote letters, and sent to Samaria, unto the rulers of Jezreel, to the elders, and to them that brought up Ahab's children, saying, Now as soon as this letter cometh to you, seeing your master's sons are with you, and there are with you chariots and horses, a fenced city also, and armour; Look even out the best and meetest of your master's sons, and set him on his father's throne, and fight for your master's house. But they were exceedingly afraid, and said, Behold, two kings stood not before him: how then shall we stand? And he that was over the house, and he that was over the city, the elders also, and the bringers up of the children, sent to Jehu, saying, We are thy servants, and will do all that thou shalt bid us; we will not make any king: do thou that which is good in thine eyes. Guidance: Jehu's letter-test. Ahab's dynasty had 70 sons (the round number for a full royal house) being raised in Samaria, the capital, by the city's officials and tutors. Jehu — having killed Joram and Jezebel at Jezreel — now moves against the rest of the line. His opening letter is a dare: 'Look out the best of your master's sons, set him on the throne, and fight for your master's house.' He challenges the Samaria establishment to mount a defense — pick a successor and resist. The challenge is a test of nerve. The Samaria leaders fail it instantly: 'Two kings stood not before him: how then shall we stand?' If Joram (with an army) and Ahaziah (king of Judah) both fell to Jehu in a day, what chance has a guardian-council? They capitulate: 'We are thy servants... we will not make any king: do that which is good in thine eyes.' The chapter is showing us how a regime collapses when its protectors calculate the odds and switch sides. The 70 heirs had every institutional advantage — chariots, horses, a fortified city, armour — but no one willing to die for them. The chapter is teaching us that human power structures are only as strong as the loyalty underneath them; when fear replaces loyalty, the strongest fortress surrenders without a fight. The chapter is also exposing the moral cowardice of the establishment: rather than defend the children they had raised, the guardians offer them up. Jehu's coup is advancing not only by his sword but by the collapse of everyone who should have resisted.

Then he wrote a letter the second time to them, saying, If ye be mine, and if ye will hearken unto my voice, take ye the heads of the men your master's sons, and come to me to Jezreel by to morrow this time. Now the king's sons, being seventy persons, were with the great men of the city, which brought them up. And it came to pass, when the letter came to them, that they took the king's sons, and slew seventy persons, and put their heads in baskets, and sent him them to Jezreel. Guidance: The second letter and the slaughter. Jehu's first letter exposed the establishment's cowardice; his second letter weaponizes it. 'If ye be mine... take ye the heads of the men your master's sons, and come to me to Jezreel by to morrow this time.' He demands proof of loyalty in the most brutal currency: the severed heads of the 70 princes they had been raising. The city's great men comply. They slaughter all 70, put the heads in baskets, and send them the 25 miles to Jezreel. The chapter is showing us the horrifying logic of complicity. The Samaria leaders had already declared themselves Jehu's servants (v5); now they prove it by killing the children entrusted to their care. By doing the killing themselves, they bind their own guilt to Jehu's cause — they cannot now turn against him, because they too have Ahab's blood on their hands. The chapter is teaching us how revolutions implicate the many in the crimes of the few: Jehu makes the whole Samaria establishment co-executioners, so that no faction can later claim innocence and rally the old loyalists. The chapter records the act with grim economy — '70 persons... heads in baskets' — and does not soften it. This is the violent reckoning the LORD had decreed against Ahab's house (1 Kgs 21:21-22, 2 Kgs 9:8), but it is executed through men whose motives are fear and self-preservation, not righteousness. The chapter holds both truths: the judgment is the LORD's; the cruelty is the men's.

And there came a messenger, and told him, saying, They have brought the heads of the king's sons. And he said, Lay ye them in two heaps at the entering in of the gate until the morning. And it came to pass in the morning, that he went out, and stood, and said to all the people, Ye be righteous: behold, I conspired against my master, and slew him: but who slew all these? Know now that there shall fall unto the earth nothing of the word of the LORD, which the LORD spake concerning the house of Ahab: for the LORD hath done that which he spake by his servant Elijah. Guidance: The two heaps and Jehu's public theology. The 70 heads arrive; Jehu orders them stacked in two heaps at the city gate — the place of public assembly — until morning. Then he stages a public address. His argument is shrewd and chilling. 'Ye be righteous' — he tells the people of Jezreel they are innocent observers. 'Behold, I conspired against my master, and slew him' — he openly owns the killing of Joram. 'But who slew all these?' He points at the 70 heads: I did not order Samaria to do this; the city's own leaders did it. The rhetorical move spreads the guilt — Jehu killed the king, but Samaria killed the princes, so the whole nation is implicated and no one can pose as the avenger of the old house. Then comes the theological frame: 'There shall fall unto the earth nothing of the word of the LORD... the LORD hath done that which he spake by his servant Elijah.' Jehu interprets the bloodbath as the precise fulfillment of Elijah's curse on Ahab's house (1 Kgs 21:21). The chapter is showing us a man who is theologically correct and morally compromised at the same time. Elijah's prophecy IS being fulfilled — that part is true. But Jehu uses true theology to launder his own ambition and to manage public opinion. The chapter is teaching us a hard discernment: the fulfillment of God's word can be real even when the human instrument's heart is impure, and a leader can quote Scripture accurately while using it to consolidate personal power. Both the divine truth and the human manipulation are present in the same speech.

So Jehu slew all that remained of the house of Ahab in Jezreel, and all his great men, and his kinsfolks, and his priests, until he left him none remaining. Guidance: The Jezreel purge completed. The narrator summarizes Jehu's mopping-up at Jezreel: he killed everyone connected to Ahab's house there — the remaining family, the courtiers ('great men'), the relatives ('kinsfolks'), and 'his priests' (likely the Baal-clergy attached to the royal court). 'Until he left him none remaining.' The phrasing echoes the curse-formula of total extinction (1 Kgs 21:21). The chapter is showing us the thoroughness of the judgment on Ahab's house — not merely the kings but the entire apparatus of the apostate regime: its political class, its bloodline, and its false priesthood. The chapter is teaching us that when the LORD judges a corrupt system, He uproots not just the figurehead but the network that sustained it. Yet the verse also marks the point where Jehu's zeal begins to exceed his commission. Hosea 1:4 will later look back and say the LORD would 'avenge the blood of Jezreel upon the house of Jehu' — indicating that the bloodshed, though it fulfilled the decree against Ahab, included excess and self-interested killing for which Jehu's own dynasty would be held accountable. The chapter holds the tension that runs through the whole Jehu narrative: the right deed done with a wrong heart and a heavy, indiscriminate hand. Obedience that overreaches into cruelty does not stay righteous.

And he arose and departed, and came to Samaria. And as he was at the shearing house in the way, Jehu met with the brethren of Ahaziah king of Judah, and said, Who are ye? And they answered, We are the brethren of Ahaziah; and we go down to salute the children of the king and the children of the queen. And he said, Take them alive. And they took them alive, and slew them at the pit of the shearing house, even two and forty men; neither left he any of them. Guidance: The 42 kinsmen of Ahaziah. Travelling from Jezreel to Samaria, Jehu stops at Beth-eked (the 'shearing house,' a place where shepherds gathered to shear flocks). There he meets a party of 42 men — relatives of Ahaziah king of Judah — who, unaware that the Omride house has just been destroyed, are travelling north 'to salute the children of the king and the children of the queen' (Joram's and Jezebel's families). They have walked straight into the purge without knowing it. Jehu orders them taken alive, then slaughtered at the pit — all 42, none spared. The chapter is showing us how the judgment on the northern Omrides spills over onto the southern Davidic relatives who had bound themselves to that house by marriage (through Athaliah). These men were of Judah's royal family, yet they died in Israel's reckoning because their loyalties and bloodlines were entangled with Ahab's. The chapter is teaching us the contagion of unholy alliances: Jehoshaphat's decision to marry his line into Ahab's (1 Kgs 22) keeps producing casualties a generation later — the southern princes die because they were going to a Baal-stained court to pay their respects. The chapter is also continuing to show Jehu's expanding, merciless momentum — he kills without inquiry, without distinction, on sight. The decree against Ahab is being carried out, but the manner is increasingly that of a man who has acquired a taste for killing.

And when he was departed thence, he lighted on Jehonadab the son of Rechab coming to meet him: and he saluted him, and said to him, Is thine heart right, as my heart is with thy heart? And Jehonadab answered, It is. If it be, give me thine hand. And he gave him his hand; and he took him up to him into the chariot. And he said, Come with me, and see my zeal for the LORD. So they made him ride in his chariot. Guidance: Jehonadab the Rechabite joins Jehu. On the road, Jehu meets Jehonadab (Jonadab) son of Rechab — the founder of the Rechabites, an austere clan that rejected wine, houses, and farming as a protest against the corrupting settled culture of Baalized Israel (their fidelity is praised centuries later in Jer 35). Jehonadab is a respected figure of strict devotion to the LORD. Jehu courts him: 'Is thine heart right, as my heart is with thy heart?' Jehonadab assents; they clasp hands; Jehu pulls him up into the chariot. 'Come with me, and see my zeal for the LORD.' The chapter is showing us Jehu acquiring religious legitimacy. By riding into Samaria with the famously godly Jehonadab beside him, Jehu publicly badges his coup as a reform-movement for the LORD, not mere ambition. The endorsement of a holy man lends moral cover to what follows. The chapter is teaching us about the word Jehu uses — 'zeal' (Hebrew qinah). It is the right word for true devotion (Phinehas had it, Elijah had it), but on Jehu's lips it is performative: he wants Jehonadab to come 'see' his zeal, as a spectacle. The chapter is also raising a quiet warning for the godly: sincere people like Jehonadab can be drawn in to legitimize the agendas of ambitious leaders who speak the language of faith. Jehonadab's heart is right; Jehu's use of him is calculated. The chapter is teaching discernment about whose chariot we climb into when 'zeal for the LORD' is the invitation.

And when he was departed thence, he lighted on Jehonadab the son of Rechab coming to meet him: and he saluted him, and said to him, Is thine heart right, as my heart is with thy heart? Jehonadab assents; they clasp hands; Jehu pulls him up into the chariot. 'Come with me, and see my zeal for the LORD.' The chapter is showing us Jehu acquiring religious legitimacy. By riding into Samaria with the famously godly Jehonadab beside him, Jehu publicly badges his coup as a reform-movement for the LORD, not mere ambition. The endorsement of a holy man lends moral cover to what follows. The chapter is teaching us about the word Jehu uses — 'zeal' (Hebrew qinah). It is the right word for true devotion (Phinehas had it, Elijah had it), but on Jehu's lips it is performative: he wants Jehonadab to come 'see' his zeal, as a spectacle. The chapter is also raising a quiet warning for the godly: sincere people like Jehonadab can be drawn in to legitimize the agendas of ambitious leaders who speak the language of faith. Jehonadab's heart is right; Jehu's use of him is calculated. The chapter is teaching discernment about whose chariot we climb into when 'zeal for the LORD' is the invitation.

And when he came to Samaria, he slew all that remained unto Ahab in Samaria, till he had destroyed him, according to the saying of the LORD, which he spake to Elijah. Guidance: The Samaria purge. Arriving at the capital, Jehu completes the extermination — killing 'all that remained unto Ahab in Samaria.' The narrator again frames it as fulfillment: 'according to the saying of the LORD, which he spake to Elijah.' Every Omride connection in the capital is eliminated; the dynasty that began with Omri and reached its corrupt height under Ahab and Jezebel is now extinct. The chapter is showing us the LORD's word arriving at its appointed terminus. What Elijah pronounced on Mount Carmel's aftermath and at Naboth's vineyard has now been carried out to the letter. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's judgments, however long deferred, are certain — Ahab had decades on the throne, his line seemed secure, and yet 'nothing of the word of the LORD' fell to the ground. The chapter is also setting up the next movement: with the political house of Ahab gone, Jehu turns to the religious institution Ahab built — the Baal cult itself. The purge of bloodlines is complete; the purge of false worship is about to begin, and it will be Jehu's most dramatic and most cunning act.

And Jehu gathered all the people together, and said unto them, Ahab served Baal a little; but Jehu shall serve him much. Now therefore call unto me all the prophets of Baal, all his servants, and all his priests; let none be wanting: for I have a great sacrifice to do to Baal; whosoever shall be wanting, he shall not live. But Jehu did it in subtilty, to the intent that he might destroy the worshippers of Baal. Guidance: The great deception announced. Jehu now stages his most audacious move. He assembles the people and declares: 'Ahab served Baal a little; but Jehu shall serve him much.' He poses as an even more devoted Baal-worshipper than Ahab was — a stunning public lie. He summons every Baal prophet, servant, and priest in the land to a 'great sacrifice,' on pain of death for any absentee. The narrator immediately exposes the trick: 'Jehu did it in subtilty, to the intent that he might destroy the worshippers of Baal.' The whole festival is a trap to gather all the Baal-clergy into one place for slaughter. The chapter is showing us a deception used in the service of a divine purge. This raises genuine moral complexity: the END (rooting out the Baal cult that had corrupted Israel) is something the LORD willed, yet the MEANS is elaborate deceit and the staging of false worship. The chapter does not present Jehu as a model of integrity; it reports what he did. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD can use even a deceiver to accomplish judgment on idolatry, while the deceiver's own character (and his retained loyalty to the golden calves, v29) remains under indictment. The chapter also exposes something about Baal-worship itself: its adherents were so eager for royal patronage that the prospect of a king out-serving Ahab drew every last one of them into the open. Idolatry that lives by power-favor is destroyed by the same craving for power-favor.

And Jehu gathered all the people together, and said unto them, Ahab served Baal a little; but Jehu shall serve him much. Now therefore call unto me all the prophets of Baal, all his servants, and all his priests; let none be wanting: for I have a great sacrifice to do to Baal; whosoever shall be wanting, he shall not live. Idolatry that lives by power-favor is destroyed by the same craving for power-favor.

And Jehu said, Proclaim a solemn assembly for Baal. And they proclaimed it. And Jehu sent through all Israel: and all the worshippers of Baal came, so that there was not a man left that came not. And they came into the house of Baal; and the house of Baal was full from one end to another. And he said unto him that was over the vestry, Bring forth vestments for all the worshippers of Baal. And he brought them forth vestments. And Jehu went, and Jehonadab the son of Rechab, into the house of Baal, and said unto the worshippers of Baal, Search, and look that there be here with you none of the servants of the LORD, but the worshippers of Baal only. And when they went in to offer sacrifices and burnt offerings, Jehu appointed fourscore men without, and said, If any of the men whom I have brought into your hands escape, he that letteth him go, his life shall be for the life of him. Guidance: The trap is set. The fake festival proceeds with chilling care. A solemn assembly is proclaimed; the summons goes through all Israel; every Baal-worshipper comes — 'not a man left that came not' — and the temple fills wall to wall. Jehu even has ceremonial vestments distributed to all of them, both to honor the occasion (deepening the deception) and to mark them out unmistakably: every man in Baal-vestments is a Baal-worshipper. Then, with Jehonadab beside him, Jehu makes a careful separation: 'Search... that there be here none of the servants of the LORD, but the worshippers of Baal only.' He ensures no faithful Israelite is accidentally caught in the temple. Finally he stations 80 armed men outside with orders that allowing any escapee will cost the guard his own life. The chapter is showing us the meticulous precision of the trap — vestments to identify the targets, a search to spare the innocent, a cordon to prevent escape. The chapter is teaching us a sobering double lesson. First, even in a deception-driven purge, there is a care taken not to destroy the LORD's true servants — the judgment is aimed, not indiscriminate, at this point. Second, the very thoroughness reveals how completely Baal-worship had been a public, institutional reality in Israel: it required a national summons to gather, and it filled a great temple. The cult that had taken a national massacre at Carmel to expose (1 Kgs 18) is here gathered, marked, and surrounded for its final reckoning.

And it came to pass, as soon as he had made an end of offering the burnt offering, that Jehu said to the guard and to the captains, Go in, and slay them; let none come forth. And they smote them with the edge of the sword; and the guard and the captains cast them out, and went to the city of the house of Baal. And they brought forth the images out of the house of Baal, and burned them. And they brake down the image of Baal, and brake down the house of Baal, and made it a draught house unto this day. Guidance: The slaughter and the desecration of Baal's temple. The moment the sacrifice ends, Jehu gives the order: 'Go in, and slay them; let none come forth.' The guards and captains fall on the assembled Baal-worshippers and put them to the sword. The bodies are cast out; then the soldiers press into the inner sanctuary ('the city of the house of Baal'), drag out the sacred images, and burn them. They break down the great pillar/image of Baal and demolish the temple itself, and — the narrator's final, deliberately contemptuous detail — 'made it a draught house unto this day.' They turned the temple of Baal into a public latrine. The chapter is showing us the total reversal of Baal's standing in Israel: the deity whose worship Jezebel made the state religion now has his clergy dead, his images burned, his temple reduced to a sewer. The desecration is intentional theology in stone: a site of the highest pagan honor becomes the lowest of unclean places. The chapter is teaching us how thoroughly the LORD dismantles institutionalized idolatry when His judgment comes — not just the people but the buildings, images, and very dignity of the false cult. The chapter is also recording a genuine and significant good: the organized worship of Baal, which had poisoned Israel since Ahab married Jezebel, is ended. Whatever Jehu's mixed motives, this act of national cleansing was real and was credited to him (v30). The temple-latrine 'unto this day' stands as a lasting monument to the impotence of the idol that could not save its own worshippers.

Thus Jehu destroyed Baal out of Israel. Howbeit from the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, who made Israel to sin, Jehu departed not from after them, to wit, the golden calves that were in Bethel, and that were in Dan. Guidance: The decisive 'howbeit.' The narrator gives Jehu his due: 'Thus Jehu destroyed Baal out of Israel.' This is a real, lasting achievement — the Tyrian Baal-cult Jezebel had imported never recovers its national footing. But the very next word turns: 'Howbeit' (a strong contrastive — yet, nevertheless). Jehu 'departed not' from the sins of Jeroboam: the golden calves at Bethel and Dan. The chapter is drawing the precise boundary of Jehu's reform. He eliminated the FOREIGN idolatry (Baal) but retained the NATIVE idolatry (Jeroboam's calf-shrines, the political-religious schism-worship that founded the northern kingdom in 1 Kgs 12). The chapter is teaching us a piercing lesson about partial reform. It is possible to attack the idolatry that is politically convenient to destroy (Baal was tied to the rival Omride dynasty Jehu overthrew) while preserving the idolatry that serves your own power (the calves were the national cult that legitimized the breakaway northern monarchy Jehu now headed). Jehu's reform reached exactly as far as his self-interest allowed and no further. The chapter is showing us that destroying someone else's idols is easy; surrendering your own is the true test of devotion. Jehu passed the first test and failed the second. The 'howbeit' is the hinge on which the whole evaluation of his reign turns: a real reformer of Baal, but never a true worshipper of the LORD with all his heart.

And the LORD said unto Jehu, Because thou hast done well in executing that which is right in mine eyes, and hast done unto the house of Ahab according to all that was in mine heart, thy children of the fourth generation shall sit on the throne of Israel. But Jehu took no heed to walk in the law of the LORD God of Israel with all his heart: for he departed not from the sins of Jeroboam, which made Israel to sin. Guidance: The reward and the indictment, side by side. The LORD's word to Jehu is remarkable for holding commendation and condemnation in a single breath. The commendation: 'Because thou hast done well in executing that which is right in mine eyes, and hast done unto the house of Ahab according to all that was in mine heart, thy children of the fourth generation shall sit on the throne of Israel.' The judgment on Ahab's house WAS the LORD's will, Jehu DID execute it, and the LORD rewards him with the longest dynasty the northern kingdom would ever have — four generations (Jehoahaz, Jehoash, Jeroboam II, Zechariah). The indictment follows immediately: 'But Jehu took no heed to walk in the law of the LORD God of Israel with all his heart: for he departed not from the sins of Jeroboam.' The chapter is teaching us one of Scripture's most carefully balanced verdicts. The LORD genuinely rewards obedience even when the obedient one is deeply flawed — Jehu gets a real, dated, four-generation promise for a real service rendered. And yet the LORD does not pretend the obedience was wholehearted. Jehu served the LORD's purpose without serving the LORD's heart. The chapter is showing us that God is both just (He rewards what was actually done right) and discerning (He sees what was withheld). It is teaching us not to mistake usefulness for godliness: a person can accomplish exactly what God decreed, be rewarded for it, and still not be walking with God 'with all his heart.' The four-generation cap on the reward is itself the limit set by the half-heartedness — a fuller obedience might have meant a fuller blessing.

In those days the LORD began to cut Israel short: and Hazael smote them in all the coasts of Israel; From Jordan eastward, all the land of Gilead, the Gadites, and the Reubenites, and the Manassites, from Aroer, which is by the river Arnon, even Gilead and Bashan. Guidance: The shrinking of Israel under Hazael. Even as Jehu reigns, the LORD 'began to cut Israel short' (Hebrew qatzah — to dock, to trim off). The instrument is Hazael of Aram — the very man Elisha had wept over (2 Kgs 8:12), now king and unleashing exactly the atrocities the prophet foresaw. Hazael strips away Israel's entire Transjordan territory: 'from Jordan eastward, all the land of Gilead' — the lands of Gad, Reuben, and the eastern half of Manasseh, from Aroer on the Arnon (the far south) through Gilead up to Bashan (the far north). These were the first tribes to settle (Num 32) and now the first to be lost. The chapter is showing us that Jehu's purge, real as it was, did not reverse the deeper national decline. Because Jehu retained the calf-worship and walked half-heartedly, the LORD's discipline on Israel continued: the kingdom begins to lose territory it had held since the conquest. The chapter is teaching us that removing the most flagrant sin (Baal) while keeping the foundational sin (the calves) does not avert national judgment — it only changes its form. The chapter is also fulfilling Elisha's tearful prophecy: Hazael, whom Jehu's reform did nothing to restrain, becomes the rod by which the LORD shortens Israel. Reform that stops short of the heart leaves a nation still exposed to the consequences of its idolatry.

In those days the LORD began to cut Israel short: and Hazael smote them in all the coasts of Israel; From Jordan eastward, all the land of Gilead, the Gadites, and the Reubenites, and the Manassites, from Aroer, which is by the river Arnon, even Gilead and Bashan. Guidance: The shrinking of Israel under Hazael. Even as Jehu reigns, the LORD 'began to cut Israel short' (Hebrew qatzah — to dock, to trim off). The instrument is Hazael of Aram — the very man Elisha had wept over (2 Kgs 8:12), now king and unleashing exactly the atrocities the prophet foresaw. Hazael strips away Israel's entire Transjordan territory: 'from Jordan eastward, all the land of Gilead' — the lands of Gad, Reuben, and the eastern half of Manasseh, from Aroer on the Arnon (the far south) through Gilead up to Bashan (the far north). These were the first tribes to settle (Num 32) and now the first to be lost. The chapter is showing us that Jehu's purge, real as it was, did not reverse the deeper national decline. Because Jehu retained the calf-worship and walked half-heartedly, the LORD's discipline on Israel continued: the kingdom begins to lose territory it had held since the conquest. The chapter is teaching us that removing the most flagrant sin (Baal) while keeping the foundational sin (the calves) does not avert national judgment — it only changes its form. The chapter is also fulfilling Elisha's tearful prophecy: Hazael, whom Jehu's reform did nothing to restrain, becomes the rod by which the LORD shortens Israel. Reform that stops short of the heart leaves a nation still exposed to the consequences of its idolatry.

Now the rest of the acts of Jehu, and all that he did, and all his might, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel? And Jehu slept with his fathers: and they buried him in Samaria. And Jehoahaz his son reigned in his stead. And the time that Jehu reigned over Israel in Samaria was twenty and eight years. Guidance: Jehu's regnal close. The standard formula closes the reign: cross-reference to the lost annals, death and burial in Samaria, succession by his son Jehoahaz, and the length of reign — 28 years (about 841-814 BC, one of the longer northern reigns). The chapter is closing the account of a consequential and deeply ambiguous king. Jehu founded the longest-lived dynasty of the northern kingdom (the promised four generations); he ended Baal-worship as a national institution; he carried out the LORD's decreed judgment on the house of Ahab. And yet he kept the golden calves, walked without his whole heart, and presided over the beginning of Israel's territorial dismemberment. The chapter is teaching us, in the quiet of a death-formula, that a life can accomplish much that is genuinely good and still fall short of the one thing required — wholehearted devotion to the LORD. Jehu is buried with honor in the capital, his dynasty secured for four generations, his name attached to a real cleansing of the land. But the prophets remember him (Hosea 1:4) as much for the blood as for the zeal. The chapter leaves us with the sober realism of Kings: God uses flawed instruments, rewards real obedience, and still holds even His instruments accountable for the heart they withheld.
